children.name: Nestor
children.description: Large, robust parrots endemic to New Zealand, with a distinctive long, decurved upper mandible that is longer in males than females. They are intelligent and inquisitive birds, known for their adaptability and sometimes destructive curiosity. Nestor species have a complex social structure and are capable of solving novel problems.

children.name: Nestor notabilis
children.description: The world's only alpine parrot, found in the mountains of New Zealand's South Island. It has olive-green plumage with brilliant orange underwings visible in flight. Renowned for its extraordinary intelligence and curiosity, the Kea is known to solve multi-step puzzles, manipulate objects, and interact playfully with humans.

children.name: Nestor meridionalis
children.description: A forest parrot found across the North and South Islands of New Zealand, with a brownish-green plumage, a grey crown, and a striking orange-red belly and underwing. It is an important seed disperser for native New Zealand trees. Populations have declined severely due to introduced mammalian predators.

children.name: Strigops
children.description: A monotypic genus of the world's heaviest parrot and the only nocturnal, flightless parrot. The Kakapo is a remarkable evolutionary anomaly, having traded flight for a terrestrial lifestyle in the predator-free environment of prehistoric New Zealand. It is a lek-breeding species with an unusually long lifespan and slow reproductive rate.

children.name: Strigops habroptila
children.description: A critically endangered, flightless, nocturnal parrot endemic to New Zealand, with mossy green plumage that provides perfect camouflage in forest undergrowth. It is the heaviest parrot in the world, weighing up to 4 kg, and has a distinctive sweet, musty odour. Intensive conservation efforts on predator-free offshore islands have brought the population back from the brink of extinction.

children.name: Cyanoramphus
children.description: Small to medium-sized parakeets endemic to New Zealand and the surrounding sub-Antarctic islands, with predominantly green plumage and a distinctive blue or red crown patch. They are active, acrobatic birds that forage in forests and shrublands for seeds, berries, and invertebrates.

children.name: Cyanoramphus unicolor
children.description: Endemic to the sub-Antarctic Antipodes Islands south of New Zealand, this is one of the most southerly-distributed parrot species. It has uniform green plumage with a dark blue crown, lacking the red markings of other Cyanoramphus parakeets. It has adapted to the harsh sub-Antarctic climate and nests in crevices in the rocky island terrain.
